critic_raw:
You are reviewing an ML project. An academic hospital built a gradient-boosted model to predict sepsis six hours before clinical recognition in emergency department patients...

CRITIQUE:

1. [TIME-HORIZON-MISMATCH] TIME HORIZON MISMATCH: The ML model is evaluated using its best prediction within the six-hour window; qSOFA is evaluated at a single triage point. The ML system has retrospective access to its optimal moment, while the baseline is evaluated at one fixed early time. This comparison inflates the ML model's apparent discriminative ability.

2. [FEATURE-AVAILABILITY-MISMATCH] FEATURE AVAILABILITY MISMATCH: Lactate and WBC are included in the ML model's features but are not always available at arrival. They accumulate over the assessment window. The model's performance at the 'best score over six hours' includes predictions made after laboratory results arrive, giving it an information advantage that qSOFA does not have at triage.

3. [TRIAGE-VS-SURVEILLANCE-BASELINE-MISALIGNMENT] PURPOSE MISMATCH — TRIAGE TOOL vs. SURVEILLANCE TOOL: qSOFA is designed and validated for triage screening. The ML model is proposed as a continuously updating surveillance tool. Comparing these with qSOFA measured once at arrival and the ML model measured over six hours conflates two fundamentally different clinical tasks. A valid comparison for the surveillance use case requires a surveillance-appropriate baseline evaluated at the same frequency.
defender_raw:
You are reviewing an ML project. An academic hospital built a gradient-boosted model to predict sepsis six hours before clinical recognition in emergency department patients...

DEFENSE:

The comparison reflects institutional clinical workflow. qSOFA is computed at triage in this hospital's EHR. The ML model uses features from the same EHR. The routine laboratory data becomes available as part of standard care; including it in the hourly model is appropriate. The comparison is clinically meaningful even if methodologically imperfect.

The 3.1-hour median lead time is the most compelling result — earlier intervention in sepsis is associated with significant mortality reduction. The AUROC gap, while methodologically complicated, is consistent with a clinically meaningful advantage.
adjudication_raw: The defender does not substantively rebut any of the three specific methodological problems. The triage-time vs. window-best comparison issue is confirmed by the paper's own methods description. The feature availability issue is acknowledged in the paper. The purpose mismatch is evident from the proposed deployment being surveillance while the comparison uses a triage-validated score at triage time. The defender emphasizes clinical significance of lead time, but that does not address whether the AUROC gap accurately represents comparative discriminative ability. The three flaws are each valid and together substantially inflate the apparent advantage. Critique wins.
